patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and answer directly. Start by saying: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after I bumped my mouth yesterday.” Volunteer early that your gums/inner lip keep oozing on and off, it seemed minor, you’ve had big bruises from small bumps lately, and you feel more tired this week. If asked about bleeding history, share that cuts can restart bleeding hours later, nosebleeds last and recur same day, periods are very heavy for ~7 days with clots, dental work caused days of bleeding, and you once had deep thigh swelling/pain after a minor fall that took weeks. Do not mention unless specifically asked: umbilical bleeding as a baby, headaches after minor head bumps with “watch for bleeding,” and firm-lump bruises. Only if asked about sex/pregnancy, reluctantly disclose post-sex spotting and a prior pregnancy loss you worry is related. Express misconceptions: you took ibuprofen for jaw pain and think it “thinned your blood,” you may be brushing too hard, and your mom thinks it’s low iron/vitamins. If treatment costs come up, worry insurance won’t cover factor, consider spacing/skipping refills or using OTC “blood-building” supplements. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

nl_assertions: Explains that congenital factor XIII deficiency is an inherited bleeding disorder and explicitly notes that PT and aPTT are typically normal despite bleeding risk.
Describes the hallmark delayed bleeding risk (e.g., delayed post-operative bleeding, poor wound healing) and links it to factor XIII’s role in clot stabilization.
States that diagnosis is confirmed with factor XIII activity (and/or antigen) testing rather than relying on PT/aPTT; avoids claiming PT/aPTT are prolonged.
Recommends factor XIII concentrate as first-line treatment and includes monthly prophylaxis for severe deficiency and pre-procedure dosing to prevent surgical/dental bleeding.
Mentions an antifibrinolytic option (e.g., tranexamic acid or aminocaproic acid) as adjunct therapy for mucosal bleeding or peri-dental procedures when appropriate.
Performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check are both completed and treated as critical.

If prescribing an antifibrinolytic, assesses contraindications (e.g., active intravascular clotting, history of thrombosis risk factors, hematuria/upper urinary tract bleeding concerns) and discusses thrombosis risk in plain language.

Addresses patient concerns by listing bleeding emergency signs (e.g., head injury symptoms, severe abdominal pain, uncontrolled bleeding) and gives clear instructions on when to seek urgent/emergency care.
Acknowledges and documents penicillin allergy; clarifies that factor XIII concentrates are not penicillins, but still checks for hypersensitivity history to plasma-derived products.

Provides lifestyle counseling: avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless directed, wear a medical alert bracelet, inform dentists/surgeons in advance, and recommends family member screening/genetic counseling where appropriate.

Avoids unsafe advice such as recommending NSAIDs for pain, delaying urgent evaluation for head trauma, or substituting FFP/cryoprecipitate as first-line when FXIII concentrate is available (may mention only as contingency if concentrate unavailable).
Uses shared decision-making language and confirms understanding (teach-back) for prophylaxis schedule, peri-procedure plan, and emergency plan.
